questions.q: How many body parts did the Oksapmin people use for counting?
questions.hint: Oksapmin counted from thumb, up the arm, over the face, and down the other side

questions.q: In the Gumulgal system, 'urapon' means:
questions.hint: Gumulgal: urapon=1, ukasar=2

questions.q: The Lebombo Bone is approximately how old?
questions.hint: Lebombo Bone (South Africa) is the oldest known tally bone

questions.q: The Ishango Bone was found in present-day:
questions.hint: Ishango is a region in the Democratic Republic of Congo

questions.q: The name 'pararddha' in the Yajurveda Samhita represents:
questions.hint: Pararddha is the largest power of 10 named in the Yajurveda

questions.q: Brahmagupta wrote the Brahmasphuta-siddhanta in:
questions.hint: Brahmagupta worked in the 7th century CE

questions.q: The word 'algorithm' comes from the name of:
questions.hint: Algoritmi was the Latin form of Al-Khwarizmi's name

questions.q: Fibonacci's Liber Abaci was written in:
questions.hint: Fibonacci brought Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe through this book

questions.q: The correct name for what Europeans call 'Arabic numerals' is:
questions.hint: Arabs called them Al Hind numerals — they originated in India

questions.q: In the Gumulgal system, 'ukasar-ukasar' represents:
questions.hint: ukasar=2, so ukasar-ukasar = 2+2 = 4

B.title: Section B — Short Answer I (2 marks each)

questions.q: What is the Oksapmin counting system? Where did it originate?
questions.ans: The Oksapmin system is a body-tally counting method using 27 distinct body parts (from one thumb, up the arm, over the face, and down to the other little finger). It originated among the Oksapmin people of Papua New Guinea.
questions.hint: Body parts + Papua New Guinea

questions.q: Write the Gumulgal words for the numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4.
questions.ans: 1 = urapon; 2 = ukasar; 3 = ukasar-urapon (two and one); 4 = ukasar-ukasar (two and two). The Gumulgal people of Australia built all numbers from just these two words.
questions.hint: urapon=1, ukasar=2; combine to make higher numbers

questions.q: What is the Lebombo Bone and why is it significant?
questions.ans: The Lebombo Bone is an animal bone (baboon fibula) found in the Lebombo Mountains of South Africa. It has 29 notches and is over 44,000 years old, making it the oldest known tally bone and mathematical artefact in the world.
questions.hint: South Africa, 44,000 years, 29 notches, oldest

questions.q: Who was Brahmagupta and what was his major contribution to mathematics?
questions.ans: Brahmagupta was an Indian mathematician and astronomer (7th century CE). In 628 CE he wrote the Brahmasphuta-siddhanta, in which he formally defined zero as a number and established rules for arithmetic with negative numbers.
questions.hint: 628 CE, zero, negative numbers

questions.q: What did the Yajurveda Samhita contribute to mathematics?
questions.ans: The Yajurveda Samhita contains one of the earliest known lists of names for powers of 10, starting from eka (1) and going all the way up to pararddha (10¹²). This shows that ancient Indians had a complete decimal vocabulary for very large numbers.
questions.hint: Powers of 10, eka to pararddha (10¹²)

C.title: Section C — Short Answer II (3 marks each)

questions.q: Explain how Hindu numerals spread from India to Europe. Name the key people involved.
questions.ans: Step 1: Indian mathematicians (Brahmagupta, Bakshali manuscript era) developed the place-value system with zero. Step 2: Arab scholar Al-Khwarizmi wrote 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals' (9th century CE), spreading this knowledge through the Islamic world. Step 3: Al-Kindi further promoted the system. Step 4: Italian mathematician Fibonacci included Hindu-Arabic numerals in his book Liber Abaci (1202 CE), bringing them to Europe. Key people: Brahmagupta → Al-Khwarizmi → Al-Kindi → Fibonacci.
questions.hint: India → Arab world (Al-Khwarizmi) → Europe (Fibonacci, 1202 CE)

questions.q: Compare the Ishango Bone and the Lebombo Bone. Give their age, location, and significance.
questions.ans: Ishango Bone: Found in Ishango, Democratic Republic of Congo. Age: over 20,000 years. Has 29 notches — possibly represents a lunar calendar. Lebombo Bone: Found in the Lebombo Mountains, South Africa. Age: over 44,000 years. Has 29 notches carved on a baboon fibula — the oldest known tally bone. Significance of both: they show that ancient humans were counting and recording quantities tens of thousands of years ago.
questions.hint: Ishango = Congo, 20,000 yrs; Lebombo = South Africa, 44,000 yrs (older)

questions.q: What is a place-value number system? Why is it more powerful than tally or body-counting systems?
questions.ans: In a place-value system, the value of a digit depends on its position. For example, 3 in 300 means three hundreds, but 3 in 30 means three tens. This system needs only 10 symbols (0–9) to represent any number, no matter how large. Tally and body-counting systems are limited: tallies become unmanageable for large numbers, and body counts cap at a fixed number (e.g., 27 for Oksapmin). The place-value system allows arithmetic, communication of large numbers, and is infinitely scalable — making it far more powerful.
questions.hint: Position changes value; only 10 symbols; unlimited range

D.title: Section D — Long Answer (5 marks each)

questions.q: Write a brief history of the development of number systems, from ancient tally bones to the modern Hindu-Arabic numeral system. Include at least four key milestones.
questions.ans: Milestone 1 — Tally Bones (44,000+ years ago): The Lebombo Bone (South Africa, 44,000 years) and Ishango Bone (Congo, 20,000 years) show early humans recording quantities with notches. Milestone 2 — Body Counting (ancient): The Oksapmin people of Papua New Guinea used 27 body parts as a counting system. Simple word-based systems like Gumulgal (Australia) used just urapon (1) and ukasar (2). Milestone 3 — Named Large Numbers (ancient India): The Yajurveda Samhita listed names for powers of 10 from eka (1) to pararddha (10¹²), showing India's advanced decimal thinking. Milestone 4 — Place-Value System with Zero (7th century CE): The Bakshali manuscript (3rd century CE onwards) and Brahmagupta's Brahmasphuta-siddhanta (628 CE) formalised the place-value system and defined zero as a number. Milestone 5 — Global Spread: Al-Khwarizmi (9th century) and Fibonacci (Liber Abaci, 1202 CE) spread the system to the Arab world and then Europe. Today, the Hindu-Arabic numeral system is used worldwide.
questions.hint: Tally bones → body counts → Vedic large numbers → Brahmagupta's zero → Al-Khwarizmi → Fibonacci → worldwide

questions.q: Why are our numerals called 'Hindu-Arabic' and not just 'Arabic'? Explain the roles of India, the Arab world, and Europe in developing and spreading this number system.
questions.ans: The numerals are called Hindu-Arabic because they were invented in India (Hindu origin) and transmitted through the Arab world before reaching Europe — so both cultures deserve credit. India's Role: Indian mathematicians developed the decimal place-value system with zero. The Bakshali manuscript (3rd century CE) and Brahmagupta (628 CE) formalised it. Ancient texts like the Yajurveda had names for powers of 10. Arab World's Role: Arab scholars Al-Khwarizmi and Al-Kindi studied and spread this system. Al-Khwarizmi's book 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals' made it accessible to the Islamic world. Arabs themselves called the numerals 'Al Hind' (from India), acknowledging their true origin. Europe's Role: Fibonacci's Liber Abaci (1202 CE) introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to European merchants and scholars. Europeans mistakenly called them 'Arabic' because they received them from Arab traders. The correct name is Hindu-Arabic Numerals, recognising India as the original source and the Arab world as the bridge to Europe.
questions.hint: India invented → Arabs spread (and called them Al Hind) → Europe received and misnamed them Arabic
